---
title: "Indicator library"
---

## Indicator library in plain English

This page translates the variable library into practical language. Users do not need to measure all indicators. Select indicators that match the site, conservation issue, capacity and decision needs.

:::{.callout-note title="Scientific interpretation"}
The indicator library is designed to support context-specific construction of monitoring systems. It protects against ecology-only monitoring by requiring representation across all domains, but it avoids a rigid universal indicator list.
:::

## Settings

| Indicator | What it means | Simple methods | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coastal land-use intensity | How much nearby land has been changed by people | Local knowledge, maps, field observations, participatory mapping | Land use can affect water quality, sedimentation and nutrient runoff |
| Sedimentation and runoff risk | Whether soil, mud or runoff is reaching the meadow | Local observations, photos after rain, Secchi depth, participatory mapping | Sediment can reduce light, smother seagrass and make restoration fail |
| Nutrient loading and water quality | Whether water has too many nutrients or poor clarity | Secchi depth, water clarity records, epiphyte estimates | Nutrients can drive algal growth and reduce light |
| Coastal development pressure | Whether building, dredging or shoreline modification may affect the meadow | Local observation, photos, policy review | Development can remove habitat or change sediment and water quality |
| Climate exposure | Whether the site is exposed to heat, storms or sea-level change | Local records, temperature loggers, climate layers | Climate shocks can affect stability and recovery |

## Resource system

| Indicator | What it means | Simple methods | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seagrass extent | How much area the meadow covers | Field mapping, GPS, snorkel surveys, citizen science | Shows whether the meadow is expanding, shrinking or stable |
| Percentage cover | How much of the seabed is covered by seagrass | Quadrats, photoquadrats, fixed transects | Core measure of ecological condition |
| Species composition | Which seagrass species are present | Field identification, photo records | Captures compositional and functional change |
| Meadow structure | Shoot density, canopy height or leaf area | Quadrats, ruler measurements | Helps explain habitat value for fish and invertebrates |
| Fragmentation and patchiness | Whether the meadow is continuous or broken up | Field sketches, GPS mapping, drones | Fragmented meadows may be more vulnerable |
| Physical disturbance | Visible damage such as anchor scars or trampling | Field observations, photographs, citizen science | Physical damage can stop recovery or cause decline |

## Resource units

| Indicator | What it means | Simple methods | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fish abundance | How many fish use the meadow | Local knowledge, catch records, visual observations | Links the meadow to biodiversity, fisheries and food |
| Fish diversity | Which fish species are present | Fisher knowledge, photo records, surveys | Shows whether the meadow supports diverse communities |
| Juvenile fish | Whether the meadow supports young fish | Fisher knowledge, simple observations, seine nets | Captures nursery function |
| Invertebrates | Shellfish, gleaned animals and small animals | Timed searches, quadrats, local knowledge | Important for food security and biodiversity |
| Megafauna use | Turtles, dugongs, rays, birds or mammals | Sightings, citizen science, local knowledge | Captures policy-relevant species supported by seagrass |
| Historical trends | How resources have changed over time | Interviews, Best Catch, oral histories | Provides temporal context where long-term data are absent |

## Actors

| Indicator | What it means | Simple methods | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Household dependence | How much people rely on seagrass for food or income | Interviews, household surveys, focus groups | Shows who may be most affected by decline or restrictions |
| Resource use patterns | How people use the meadow | Interviews, user counts, participatory mapping | Helps explain pressure and management needs |
| Adaptive capacity | Whether people have options if access or ecology changes | Household surveys, interviews | Determines vulnerability to ecological or regulatory change |
| Local knowledge | Knowledge of seasons, change, drivers and values | Interviews, focus groups, participatory mapping | Reveals trends not captured by short-term datasets |
| Stewardship | How people care for or monitor the meadow | Project records, questionnaires | Supports long-term conservation durability |
| Place attachment | How people feel connected to the place | Interviews, focus groups | Non-material values can shape support |
| Awareness and attitudes | What people know and feel about seagrass and rules | Questionnaires, interviews | Helps explain behaviour and compliance |
| Trust and social capital | Whether people trust managers and each other | Surveys, focus groups | Affects cooperation and legitimacy |

## Governance system

| Indicator | What it means | Simple methods | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Management rules | Whether rules exist for activities affecting seagrass | Policy review, management plans, interviews | Rules shape whether pressures can be managed |
| Access and tenure | Who can use, manage or restrict access | Participatory mapping, key informants | Determines who benefits and who bears costs |
| Participation | Whether affected people are included in decisions | Meeting records, interviews | Affects legitimacy and fairness |
| Enforcement capacity | Whether rules can be followed and enforced | Manager interviews, household surveys | Rules may not change behaviour without enforcement |
| Cross-sector coordination | Whether agencies work together | Institutional mapping, key informants | Seagrass pressures often come from multiple sectors |
| Conflict resolution | Whether disputes can be managed fairly | Focus groups, governance review | Conservation can create or reveal conflict |
| Financing and capacity | Whether monitoring and management can continue | Project records, organisational assessment | Monitoring often fails when funding ends |
| Data governance and ethics | Who owns, controls and benefits from data | Consent processes, protocol review | Critical when using community knowledge |

## Interactions

| Indicator | What it means | Simple methods | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fishing and gleaning pressure | How much extractive use occurs | Interviews, catch monitoring, participatory mapping | Fishing and gleaning can be livelihoods and pressures |
| Boating and anchoring pressure | How boats and anchors affect the meadow | User counts, observations, drone surveys | Captures direct physical damage |
| Restoration effort | What restoration activity has taken place | Project records, spatial records | Outcomes cannot be interpreted without effort data |
| Behaviour change | Whether people are changing practices | Surveys, observation, project records | Many interventions work only if behaviour changes |
| Displacement effects | Whether pressure moved elsewhere | Interviews, participatory mapping, nearby monitoring | Avoids false success at one site |
| Conflict and cooperation | Whether users cooperate or conflict | Focus groups, interviews | Social stability affects durability |
| Unintended consequences | Unexpected effects or trade-offs | Interviews, focus groups, reflection sessions | Reveals system responses beyond intended outputs |

## Outcomes

| Indicator | What it means | Simple methods | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seagrass condition trajectory | Whether condition is improving or declining | Repeat EOV monitoring, mapping | Core ecological outcome |
| Biodiversity and function | Whether the meadow supports ecological life | Faunal surveys, local knowledge | A meadow can exist without fully recovering function |
| Resource availability | Whether used resources are changing | Catch monitoring, local knowledge | Links ecology to provisioning benefits |
| Food security | Whether seagrass supports diets and food access | Household surveys, interviews | Key outcome in dependent systems |
| Livelihood outcomes | Effects on income or stability | Livelihood surveys, interviews | Conservation can affect economic wellbeing |
| Wellbeing | Effects on pride, identity or quality of life | Questionnaires, focus groups | Not all values are financial |
| Equity and fairness | Whether costs and benefits are fair | Focus groups, disaggregated surveys | Unfair conservation may not last |
| Compliance and legitimacy | Whether rules are supported and followed | Surveys, observations | Durable management depends on legitimacy |
| Adaptive management response | Whether monitoring leads to action | Management records, policy review | Monitoring should inform decisions |
